name: Code Review Skills
description: Level up your code reviews — what to look for, how to give feedback, and common red flags in frontend code.

cards.front: What makes a good code review?
cards.back:
A good review is:

• Timely — don't block the team for days
• Kind — critique code, not the person
• Thorough — check logic, edge cases, readability
• Educational — explain *why* something is a problem
• Proportional — don't nitpick trivial style in a critical bugfix

Goal: catch bugs, share knowledge, and improve code quality.
Anti-goal: gatekeeping or showing off.

cards.front: What should you look at FIRST in a PR? (Big picture)
cards.back:
Before reading any code, check:

• PR description — does it explain *what* and *why*?
• Size — PRs over ~400 lines are hard to review well
• File list — do the changed files make sense for the task?
• Architecture — does the approach fit the codebase?
• Tests — are they included?

Red flag: A PR that touches 20 unrelated files.
Green flag: Small, focused PR with a clear description.

cards.front: What common React anti-patterns should you flag in code review?
cards.back:
Watch for:

• Prop drilling through 4+ levels → use context or composition
• Huge god-components (300+ lines) → split into smaller pieces
• Business logic inside JSX → extract to hooks or utils
• Derived state stored in useState → compute it during render instead
• useEffect for things that aren't side effects → often a sign of over-engineering
• Index as key in dynamic lists → causes subtle re-render bugs
• Inline object/array literals in props → new ref every render

cards.front: How do you spot performance issues in React code during review?
cards.back:
Look for:

• Missing keys or index-as-key on lists
• New object/function refs created every render in props
• Large components that re-render on unrelated state changes
• Missing useMemo/useCallback where expensive work is repeated
• Fetching data in useEffect without cleanup or caching
• Unnecessary re-renders from context — wrapping too much in one provider
• Importing entire libraries (e.g., all of lodash) instead of tree-shakeable imports

Tip: Not every render is a problem. Only flag when it causes measurable slowness.

cards.front: When should you Request Changes vs Approve vs Comment?
cards.back:
• Approve — code is solid, or issues are trivial (typos, nits)
• Approve with comments — minor suggestions, trust the author to handle them
• Comment only — questions or discussion points, not blocking
• Request changes — bugs, security issues, broken logic, missing tests for critical paths

Rule of thumb:
→ Would you be okay if this shipped as-is? Approve.
→ Could this cause a bug or outage? Request changes.
→ Unsure? Comment and ask questions.

Don't request changes for style preferences.

cards.front: How do you write helpful code review comments?
cards.back:
Good comments:

• Explain the WHY, not just "change this"
• Use prefixes: nit:, suggestion:, question:, blocker:
• Offer alternatives — show a code snippet if possible
• Are specific — "this will break when X is null" > "looks wrong"
• Acknowledge good work — "nice refactor here 👍"

Bad comments:
• "This is wrong." (no explanation)
• "I would have done it differently." (not actionable)
• "Why?" with no context

Tone test: Would you say this to someone's face?

cards.front: What should you check when reviewing for accessibility (a11y)?
cards.back:
Check for:

• Semantic HTML — <button> not <div onClick>, <nav>, <main>, <section>
• Alt text on images (meaningful, not "image.png")
• Keyboard navigation — can you Tab through interactive elements?
• ARIA labels on icon-only buttons and custom widgets
• Color contrast — don't rely on color alone to convey info
• Focus management — modals should trap focus, route changes should announce
• Form labels — every input needs a visible or aria-label

Quick test: Can a screen reader user complete the feature?

cards.front: When reviewing TypeScript types, what should you check?
cards.back:
Look for:

• `any` usage — almost always a red flag, ask for a real type
• Overly loose types — string when it should be a union like 'success' | 'error'
• Missing return types on exported functions
• Type assertions (as Foo) — hiding real type errors?
• Duplicated types — should they use a shared interface?
• Optional props that are actually required in practice
• Generic types that are too complex — could it be simpler?

Good sign: Types that document intent and prevent impossible states.

cards.front: What security red flags should you catch in frontend code review?
cards.back:
Flag immediately:

• dangerouslySetInnerHTML without sanitization → XSS risk
• User input used directly in URLs or DOM
• Secrets or API keys hardcoded in client code
• eval() or new Function() with dynamic input
• Disabling CORS protections or security headers
• Auth tokens stored in localStorage (prefer httpOnly cookies)
• Missing input validation before API calls
• Regex from user input (ReDoS attacks)

Rule: Never trust user input. Ever. Even from your own forms.

cards.front: What are code smells in component architecture?
cards.back:
Watch for:

• God component — one file does fetching, state, logic, and UI
• Boolean prop explosion — <Modal isOpen isLarge isAnimated isDark />
• Tight coupling — component reaches into another's internal state
• Wrapper hell — 5+ levels of providers/wrappers for one feature
• Copy-paste components — 2 components that are 90% identical
• Smart components at leaf level — data fetching in a Button?
• Props that just pass through unchanged (prop drilling)

Fix: Separate data/logic (hooks) from presentation (components).

cards.front: What should you look for when reviewing CSS?
cards.back:
Check for:

• Magic numbers — width: 347px; → why that number?
• !important — usually a specificity hack, find the root cause
• Fixed heights on text containers — will break with longer content
• Missing responsive styles — does it work on mobile?
• Z-index wars — random z-index: 9999 without a scale
• Hardcoded colors — should use design tokens or theme variables
• Layout hacks — negative margins, absolute positioning for layout
• Unused styles — dead CSS adds bundle size

Good sign: Uses design system tokens, logical properties, and container queries.

cards.front: How do you review tests — are they actually testing anything useful?
cards.back:
Watch for:

• Tests that just check "it renders" — that's not a real test
• No assertions — test passes but verifies nothing
• Testing implementation details — checking internal state or method calls
• Snapshot-only tests — will be blindly updated, rarely catch bugs
• Missing edge cases — only happy path, no error states
• Mocking everything — if you mock the whole system, what are you testing?

Good tests:
• Test user-visible behavior, not implementation
• Cover happy path + error path + edge cases
• Are readable as documentation of what the feature does

cards.front: What naming and readability issues should you flag?
cards.back:
Flag:

• Single-letter variables outside tiny loops — data, val, x
• Boolean names without is/has/should — enabled vs isEnabled
• Misleading names — handleClick that actually submits a form
• Inconsistent conventions — fetchUser vs getUser vs loadUser in same codebase
• Abbreviated names — usr, btn, mgr → just spell it out
• Functions over 40 lines — probably doing too much
• Deeply nested logic (3+ levels) — extract into named functions

Litmus test: Could a new team member understand this without asking you?

cards.front: What state management red flags should you watch for?
cards.back:
Flag:

• Duplicated state — same data in useState AND Redux/context
• Derived state in useState — compute it from source of truth instead
• Global state for local concerns — form input state in Redux?
• State updates in useEffect that trigger more useEffects → cascade
• No loading/error states — what happens when the API fails?
• Stale closures — useEffect/callback using outdated state
• Premature state management library — useState + context handles most apps

Principle: State should have a single source of truth and live as close to where it's used as possible.

cards.front: How should you handle disagreements during code review?
cards.back:
Do:

• Assume good intent — they might know something you don't
• Ask questions first — "What was the reasoning here?" before "This is wrong"
• Distinguish preference from correctness — if both work, let the author choose
• Move to a call if a thread has 3+ back-and-forth replies
• Cite docs, benchmarks, or examples — not just opinion
• Agree on team standards beforehand to avoid repeat debates

Don't:
• Die on every hill — save strong opinions for things that matter
• Make it personal — it's about the code, not the coder
• Block a PR over style preferences
